bins.name: No cost shifting to consumers
bins.desc: OCC insists that large loads bear 100% of interconnection and upgrade costs. Crediting mechanisms and regional cost allocation must not be used for facilities built primarily to serve private large loads, and any cost shift requires demonstrated net public benefit. This aligns with ICC I and ICC II cost causation precedent.
quotes: this proposal presents substantial risks to residential consumers, who could bear higher costs and diminished oversight as transmission policy shifts to accommodate extraordinary private demand growth from large industrial and data center loads.
such "credits" frequently evolve into generalized cost socialization—resulting in rate impacts borne by retail residential customers who do not benefit from the facilities being built.
Prohibit the use of transmission credits, rate-based recovery, or regional cost allocation mechanisms for interstate transmission facilities built primarily to serve large private loads

Ensure that transmission cost recovery aligns with current cost causation and cost allocation principles developed in Illinois Commerce Commission v. FERC, 576 F.3d 470, 476 (7th Cir. 2009) (ICC I) ("FERC is not authorized to approve a pricing scheme that requires a group of utilities to pay for facilities from which its members derive no benefits, or benefits that are trivial in relation to the costs sought to be shifted to its members.")

Annual reporting on the consumer cost impacts of large-load interconnections, associated transmission expansions, and the impact on electricity and capacity markets rates for consumers other than large loads.
The Commission should affirm that no interconnection right or transmission upgrade will be granted unless the applicant bears 100% of the costs of interconnection-related costs.
The cost of obtaining replacement capacity should not be borne by existing consumers who have relied on and been paying for the suspension resources for many years.
Consumers should not bear the financial or reliability burdens of speculative industrial growth.

bins.name: Prohibit credit and cost recovery mechanisms
bins.desc: OCC calls for an express prohibition on transmission credits, rate-based recovery, and regional cost allocation for large-load upgrade facilities, citing the risk that credits evolve into broad cost socialization and violate established cost causation case law.
quotes: such "credits" frequently evolve into generalized cost socialization—resulting in rate impacts borne by retail residential customers who do not benefit from the facilities being built.
Prohibit the use of transmission credits, rate-based recovery, or regional cost allocation mechanisms for interstate transmission facilities built primarily to serve large private loads

Ensure that transmission cost recovery aligns with current cost causation and cost allocation principles developed in Illinois Commerce Commission v. FERC, 576 F.3d 470, 476 (7th Cir. 2009) (ICC I) ("FERC is not authorized to approve a pricing scheme that requires a group of utilities to pay for facilities from which its members derive no benefits, or benefits that are trivial in relation to the costs sought to be shifted to its members.")
The Commission should affirm that no interconnection right or transmission upgrade will be granted unless the applicant bears 100% of the costs of interconnection-related costs.

bins.name: Mandatory public disclosure
bins.desc: OCC wants all interconnection requests above 20 MW, cost responsibility determinations, and annual consumer cost impact reports posted publicly. Without these disclosures, consumers risk subsidizing projects with little public benefit.
quotes: Consumers, state advocates and state regulatory commissions have a right to know which entities are seeking large-load interconnections, the nature of the requested service, and how related costs could flow through to transmission and distribution rates.
All interconnection requests above 20 MW, including project type and location

Annual reporting on the consumer cost impacts of large-load interconnections, associated transmission expansions, and the impact on electricity and capacity markets rates for consumers other than large loads.

bins.name: Limit federal jurisdictional reach
bins.desc: OCC argues the ANOPR overstates FERC's jurisdiction and ignores FPA sections 212(g) and (h) that limit federal regulation of direct retail connections. Federal rules must coordinate with state commissions and must not preempt state retail tariff authority.
quotes: A rule that allows direct transmission interconnections for large end-use customers without coordination with state commissions could undermine local reliability and accountability.
FERC's rule must not undercut or preempt state efforts to manage large-load growth through retail tariffs and rate design.

The ANOPR makes no mention of the prohibitions in FPA sections 212(g) and (h) on federal regulation of retail marketing areas and of federal regulations "concerning the transmission of electric energy directly to an ultimate consumer."

Large load interconnections to any interstate transmission facility, no matter how local in nature from a planning perspective, should not escape Commission review under the ANOPR and any proposed or final rules on this matter.

bins.name: Preserve state regulatory authority
bins.desc: OCC urges FERC to expressly confirm that states retain authority over retail tariffs and service conditions for large-load customers, citing Ohio's Large Data Center Tariff as an example of state cost-reliability balancing that a federal rule must not displace or duplicate.
quotes: A rule that allows direct transmission interconnections for large end-use customers without coordination with state commissions could undermine local reliability and accountability.
FERC's rule must not undercut or preempt state efforts to manage large-load growth through retail tariffs and rate design.
Ohio's Large Data Center Tariff was developed through state regulatory proceedings to balance the interests of high-load customers with system costs and reliability for existing retail consumers.

The ANOPR makes no mention of the prohibitions in FPA sections 212(g) and (h) on federal regulation of retail marketing areas and of federal regulations "concerning the transmission of electric energy directly to an ultimate consumer."

bins.name: PJM planning gap for local facilities
bins.desc: OCC notes that in PJM and other RTO regions, local or non-regional facilities fall outside regional planning review even though they represent more than 75% of new transmission investment costs. Large load interconnections to any interstate facility, including local ones, must be subject to FERC review.
quotes: Ohio's Large Data Center Tariff was developed through state regulatory proceedings to balance the interests of high-load customers with system costs and reliability for existing retail consumers.

Large load interconnections to any interstate transmission facility, no matter how local in nature from a planning perspective, should not escape Commission review under the ANOPR and any proposed or final rules on this matter.

bins.name: Scaled deposits to deter speculation
bins.desc: OCC supports non-refundable, project-size-scaled study deposits and withdrawal penalties to discourage speculative queue entries, which distort demand forecasts and trigger premature network upgrades recovered from consumers.
quotes: speculative filings- particularly by unconfirmed data center and industrial developers- have contributed to inaccurate demand forecasts and queue congestion, which can lead to unnecessary or premature network upgrades later recovered from consumers.
OCC also supports scaling study deposits and readiness milestones to project size, with non-refundable deposits and withdrawal penalties to discourage speculative queue participation.

bins.name: Clustered study for cumulative impacts
bins.desc: OCC opposes purely serial interconnection studies, which fragment planning and overlook cumulative impacts. A coordinated or clustered study framework should evaluate all large-load and hybrid requests together, and all facilities including local ones must be subject to Commission review.
quotes: speculative filings- particularly by unconfirmed data center and industrial developers- have contributed to inaccurate demand forecasts and queue congestion, which can lead to unnecessary or premature network upgrades later recovered from consumers.

Large load interconnections to any interstate transmission facility, no matter how local in nature from a planning perspective, should not escape Commission review under the ANOPR and any proposed or final rules on this matter.

the RTO or regional planning authority should perform a forward-looking resource adequacy assessment- spanning at least three years – to determine whether the suspension would erode reserve margins or necessitate additional capacity procurement.

bins.name: Retain 20 MW federal threshold
bins.desc: OCC supports a defined 20 MW minimum threshold to limit federal jurisdiction to the largest transmission-connected loads, preserve state oversight of smaller customers, and require public posting for requests at or above that level.
quotes: All interconnection requests above 20 MW, including project type and location
OCC supports retaining a defined minimum threshold such as 20 MW for purposes of any federal interconnection standard.

bins.name: Expedited process with firm safeguards
bins.desc: OCC recognizes potential value in expedited processing for curtailable loads but requires that any such track include consumer cost protections, enforceable curtailment commitments verified through telemetry, and financial penalties for non-performance.
quotes: any expedited connection process must be designed to protect consumers from inequitable queue treatment, hidden reliability costs, or cost shifting.
any expedited processing be contingent on enforceable curtailment and dispatchability commitment verified through telemetry and subject to penalties for non-performance.

bins.name: Curtailability must be enforceable
bins.desc: OCC supports flexible interconnection rights for curtailable loads only when backed by telemetry verification and real penalties. Without enforcement, curtailable projects could behave as firm load while receiving preferential queue treatment.
quotes: any expedited connection process must be designed to protect consumers from inequitable queue treatment, hidden reliability costs, or cost shifting.
any expedited processing be contingent on enforceable curtailment and dispatchability commitment verified through telemetry and subject to penalties for non-performance.

bins.name: Operational limits on hybrid facilities
bins.desc: OCC calls for automated metering, telemetry caps, and mandatory penalties to prevent hybrid facilities from using the transmission system for arbitrage or unaccounted transactions that shift balancing and congestion costs to other customers.
quotes: any expedited processing be contingent on enforceable curtailment and dispatchability commitment verified through telemetry and subject to penalties for non-performance.

Hybrid facilities that both draw and inject energy at a single interconnection point must not be allowed to use the transmission system for arbitrage or unaccounted energy transactions that shift balancing and congestion costs to other customers.

bins.name: Resource adequacy review for suspended generation
bins.desc: OCC supports requiring a two-part SSR/RMR analysis plus a three-year forward resource adequacy assessment when generation capacity suspends to serve new load, with replacement capacity costs borne by the interconnecting customer rather than existing consumers.
quotes: the RTO or regional planning authority should perform a forward-looking resource adequacy assessment- spanning at least three years – to determine whether the suspension would erode reserve margins or necessitate additional capacity procurement.
The cost of obtaining replacement capacity should not be borne by existing consumers who have relied on and been paying for the suspension resources for many years.

bins.name: Deter speculative queue participation
bins.desc: OCC supports requiring demonstrated technical and financial viability, including site control and financing commitments, before applicants enter the study process, to prevent inaccurate demand forecasts and queue congestion.
quotes: speculative filings- particularly by unconfirmed data center and industrial developers- have contributed to inaccurate demand forecasts and queue congestion, which can lead to unnecessary or premature network upgrades later recovered from consumers.
OCC also supports scaling study deposits and readiness milestones to project size, with non-refundable deposits and withdrawal penalties to discourage speculative queue participation.

bins.name: Consumer protection as core FERC duty
bins.desc: OCC argues that FERC's primary duty under the FPA is just, reasonable, and non-discriminatory rates, and that the ANOPR's focus on facilitating AI innovation and industrial expansion risks reframing that mission away from consumer protection.
quotes: this proposal presents substantial risks to residential consumers, who could bear higher costs and diminished oversight as transmission policy shifts to accommodate extraordinary private demand growth from large industrial and data center loads.
Consumers should not bear the financial or reliability burdens of speculative industrial growth.

bins.name: Prevent hybrid arbitrage cost shifting
bins.desc: OCC wants separate metering for inflows and outflows, automated equipment capping injections and withdrawals to approved rights, and public reporting of hybrid facility performance to uphold cost causation and system reliability.
quotes: Hybrid facilities that both draw and inject energy at a single interconnection point must not be allowed to use the transmission system for arbitrage or unaccounted energy transactions that shift balancing and congestion costs to other customers.
